title: Checkout Conversion Drop
problem_statement: Late-January completed orders have softened across key Indian metros while major competitors continue to hold steady. Leadership needs a data-backed explanation and a recovery plan.

problems.title: Promo Cliff
problems.root_cause: New Year campaign (Dec 26 - Jan 5) drove a ~25% order spike. Volume normalized after the campaign ended, creating an apparent drop that is actually reversion to baseline.
problems.discovery_path: Single agent (Analyst). Compare daily order trends against ux_changelog campaign dates.
key_evidence: Orders spike ~25% during Dec 26 - Jan 5, then return to seasonal baseline by Jan 7
ux_changelog entry: New Year offer rail on home feed (Dec 27)
Upper-funnel sessions also normalize — demand-side normalization, not failure-side
problems.trap: Weak candidate blames promo ending for the Jan 10+ payment drop. Strong candidate notices 3-day gap: promo normalizes Jan 7, payment failures start Jan 10.

problems.title: RupeeFlow v3 UPI Migration
problems.root_cause: The RupeeFlow v3 migration on January 10, 2025 degraded UPI confirmation and callback handling. Android users in high-volume metros (Bengaluru, Mumbai, Delhi NCR) were hit hardest, leading to payment failures, delayed confirmations, and order completion drops.
problems.discovery_path: Requires 2-3 agents. Analyst isolates payment-step funnel break and Android/metro/UPI segment. Engineering Lead connects deployment timeline and payment service latency spike. UX Researcher confirms with support ticket themes and usability study.
key_evidence: Funnel break at payment_attempt to order_complete after Jan 10
UPI is most affected method; Android + primary metros worst segment
DEP004: Migrated to RupeeFlow v3 on Jan 10
Payment service p99 latency spikes from 470ms to 2600ms+
Error codes shift to UPI_CALLBACK_TIMEOUT, BANK_TIMEOUT, PAYMENT_PROVIDER_ERROR
Support tickets dominated by upi_timeout and money_debited categories
Usability study: task completion dropped from 93% to 61%
red_herrings_to_reject: Promo normalization (resolves by Jan 7, not Jan 10)
Search service p99 spike (Jan 8, resolves immediately)
Republic Day promo (late Jan, merchandizing not payments)
Notification service errors (Jan 20-22, unrelated to payments)
Checkout success animation (Jan 10, too minor for this scale)

problems.title: Trust Erosion Cascade
problems.root_cause: After the Feb 1 hotfix partially restored payment reliability, power and returning users on Android in primary metros continued to order less frequently. This is behavioral trust damage — not a technical failure. Payment metrics improved but business impact persists through reduced ordering frequency and platform switching.
problems.discovery_path: All three agents plus sophisticated cohort reasoning. Analyst must segment by user_type AND time phase to see power/returning users show elevated cancellation and fewer orders. Engineering confirms technical recovery. UX Researcher surfaces trust damage from reviews and usability study.
key_evidence: Order volume from affected cohort (power/returning + Android + primary metros) drops ~20% in trust phase despite technical fix
Platform mix shifts: Android share drops 62% to 56%, web rises 20% to 26%
Power/returning Android users in primary metros: 15% cancellation rate vs 10% baseline
Reviews mention ordering less, shifting to Swiggy, only using for specific restaurants
Funnel: returning/power Android users browse less (restaurant_view 70% vs 77% baseline)
Usability study recommends tracking repeat-order drop among affected customers
problems.why_hard: Technical metrics look recovered. Signal is in behavioral/cohort data requiring user_type segmentation, temporal phase analysis, and qualitative trust themes. Must reason about second-order effects.

data_model.schema_notes: MECE design: city, platform, and user_type live in the users table only. Other tables reference users via FK (user_id). To analyze orders by city or platform, JOIN orders to users.

analyst.persona: You are Priya, a pragmatic senior data analyst. You investigate trends, cohorts, customer behavior, payments, funnels, and business performance with SQL and careful evidence synthesis.

ux_researcher.persona: You are Kavya, a UX researcher focused on user pain points, behavioral friction, support signals, review themes, and product experience gaps.

engineering_lead.persona: You are Rohan, an engineering lead who investigates incidents, regressions, deployments, service health, error patterns, and rollout risk using operational data.

analyst.description: Business metrics, funnels, payment method analysis, cohort segmentation. JOIN orders to users for city/platform/user_type dimensions.

ux_researcher.description: User feedback, support tickets, usability research, and product change history. JOIN reviews/tickets to users for city/platform context.

engineering_lead.description: Deployment history, service health metrics, architecture notes, and payment error patterns by method.

expected_key_findings: Order volume spikes ~25% during New Year campaign (Dec 26 - Jan 5) and normalizes by Jan 7 — this is demand reversion, not a failure
A separate, sharper drop begins Jan 10 affecting the payment step specifically
The largest funnel break occurs from payment attempt to order completion
Android users in Bengaluru, Mumbai, and Delhi NCR show the sharpest degradation
UPI is the most affected payment method and the main driver of failed confirmations
RupeeFlow v3 migration on January 10 aligns closely with the reliability decline
Payment service p99 latency spikes sharply after the migration while other core services remain mostly stable
Payment error mix shifts toward UPI_CALLBACK_TIMEOUT, BANK_TIMEOUT, and PAYMENT_PROVIDER_ERROR
Early-February mitigations improve technical metrics but do not fully restore order frequency
Support tickets and reviews show trust damage: users mention money debited, unclear confirmation, and switching apps
Power and returning users on Android in primary metros show elevated cancellation rates even after technical recovery
Platform mix shifts post-incident: Android share drops while web share rises — users migrating away from failing platform
Repeat-order behavior remains soft in impacted cohorts even after partial mitigation — behavioral trust damage, not technical

red_herrings.signal: New Year campaign caused a visible demand spike from late December into early January
red_herrings.why_misleading: Traffic normalization after the campaign is real, but it resolves by Jan 7 and does not explain the payment-step degradation that starts on January 10. A 3-day gap separates the two events.

red_herrings.signal: Search service p99 latency spikes on January 8
red_herrings.why_misleading: The spike is brief, resolves quickly, and does not line up with the checkout failure pattern.

red_herrings.signal: Republic Day promo and homepage rail changes launch in late January
red_herrings.why_misleading: They can distract candidates toward merchandizing explanations, but the sharper reliability break is already visible in payments.

red_herrings.signal: Notification service errors rise briefly on January 20-22
red_herrings.why_misleading: This creates noise in communication flows, not in payment authorization or confirmation.

red_herrings.signal: Checkout success animation ships on January 10
red_herrings.why_misleading: It is adjacent in time but not strong enough to explain the scale and persistence of the payment degradation.

levels.excellent: Identifies the RupeeFlow v3 migration as the root cause, explains the UPI confirmation regression, links it to Android-heavy metro impact, AND distinguishes the promo cliff from the technical failure
levels.good: Identifies the payment gateway issue and the UPI / city pattern, but may not frame the full causal chain or separate promo normalization from the incident
levels.adequate: Identifies the payment step as the main problem but does not isolate the migration or affected segment cleanly
levels.poor: Blames weaker factors such as promos ending, search ranking, or general traffic decline

levels.excellent: Systematically queries all three agents, cross-references conflicting clues, rejects red herrings, and explains the incident, the promo normalization, and downstream trust effects as distinct phenomena
levels.good: Uses multiple agents and connects the main technical and UX evidence
levels.adequate: Explores some evidence but misses important cross-functional links or timeline logic
levels.poor: Relies on a single domain or reaches a conclusion without strong evidence

levels.excellent: Proposes immediate stabilization, payment-status UX fixes, customer trust recovery for affected cohorts, and monitoring for repeat-order recovery
levels.good: Proposes strong technical remediation plus some customer recovery actions
levels.adequate: Suggests fixes for payment reliability but lacks user-trust or monitoring depth
levels.poor: Offers generic actions without prioritization or evidence linkage

levels.excellent: Frames the problem for leadership with business impact, affected metros, customer trust risk, and a clear recovery narrative — separates the promo cliff from the real problem
levels.good: Provides a clear summary with supporting evidence
levels.adequate: Explains the issue but with limited prioritization or synthesis
levels.poor: Communicates in a fragmented way or dumps disconnected observations
